readme: # Posthog JS This is a monorepo containing multiple packages to integrate with Posthog using JavaScript! ## Documentation - [Posthog JS Documentation](https://posthog.com/docs/libraries/js) ## Packages - [posthog-js](./packages/browser/README.md) - [posthog-js-lite](./packages/web/README.md) - [posthog-node](./packages/node/README.md) - [posthog-react-native](./packages/react-native/README.md) - [@posthog/react](./packages/react/README.md) - [@posthog/ai](./packages/ai/README.md) - [@posthog/convex](./packages/convex/README.md) - [@posthog/nextjs-config](./packages/nextjs-config/README.md) - [@posthog/nuxt](./packages/nuxt/README.md) - [@posthog/plugin-utils](./packages/plugin-utils/README.md) - [@posthog/rollup-plugin](./packages/rollup-plugin/README.md) - [@posthog/types](./packages/types/README.md) - [@posthog/webpack-plugin](./packages/webpack-plugin/README.md) ## Contributing See [CONTRIBUTING.md](./CONTRIBUTING.md)

chore: bump @posthog/cli to ~0.7.13 (#3661)

@posthog/cli 0.7.13 drops several unused runtime dependencies (axios,
axios-proxy-builder, console.table, rimraf). Bump the catalog entry and
prune the now-orphaned packages from the lockfile.

Generated-By: PostHog Code
Task-Id: ac208e0b-1e2e-4b6f-9c89-3fb0633ea2bc

prs.body:
## Summary

- Bumps `@posthog/cli` from `~0.7.3` to `~0.7.13` in the workspace catalog.
- 0.7.13 drops several unused runtime dependencies (`axios`, `axios-proxy-builder`, `console.table`, `rimraf` and their transitives like `tunnel`, `easy-table`, `glob@13`, `path-scurry`), so the lockfile is also pruned.
- Adds a changeset for the four consumers: `@posthog/nextjs-config`, `@posthog/nuxt`, `@posthog/rollup-plugin`, `@posthog/webpack-plugin`.

## Test plan

- [ ] `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile` passes locally
- [ ] CI for the affected packages stays green
prs.title: chore: bump @posthog/cli to ~0.7.13

prs.body:
## Problem

Two related bugs in feature flag event handling:

1. In the Node SDK, `$feature_flag_called` events were not fired for the same flag+variant combination when evaluated under different group contexts — the deduplication key did not account for groups, so only the first call would fire an event.
2. In the core SDK, bootstrap flag variants could be silently overridden when the server returned a boolean response for the same flag key, losing the variant information provided at bootstrap time.

## Changes

- Node SDK: group context is now included in the `$feature_flag_called` deduplication key, so events fire independently per group combination.
- Core SDK: when merging server flag results, bootstrap flag values take precedence to preserve variant data over a server boolean response.

## Release info Sub-libraries affected

### Libraries affected

<!-- Please mark which libraries will require a version bump. -->

- [ ] All of them
- [x] posthog-js (web)
- [ ] posthog-js-lite (web lite)
- [x] posthog-node
- [ ] posthog-react-native
- [ ] @posthog/react
- [ ] @posthog/ai
- [ ] @posthog/convex
- [ ] @posthog/next
- [ ] @posthog/nextjs-config
- [ ] @posthog/nuxt
- [ ] @posthog/rollup-plugin
- [ ] @posthog/webpack-plugin
- [ ] @posthog/types

## Checklist

- [x] Tests for new code
- [x] Accounted for the impact of any changes across different platforms
- [x] Accounted for backwards compatibility of any changes (no breaking changes!)
- [ ] Took care not to unnecessarily increase the bundle size

### If releasing new changes

- [ ] Ran `pnpm changeset` to generate a changeset file

<!-- For more details check RELEASING.md -->

## How did you test this code?

New unit tests added in `evaluate-flags.spec.ts` (Node SDK) and `posthog.featureflags.spec.ts` (core SDK) covering both scenarios.

👉 _Stay up-to-date with [PostHog coding conventions](https://posthog.com/docs/contribute/coding-conventions) for a smoother review._
prs.title: fix: feature flag called deduplication and bootstrap variant preservation

prs.body:
## Problem

Session recording event compression still used synchronous `fflate` gzip on the main thread, even though the browser SDK request path can use native async `CompressionStream` gzip when available.

## Changes

- Use `@posthog/core`'s `gzipCompress` / `isGzipSupported` for session recording event compression when native async gzip is available.
- Preserve replay event ordering by queueing events while async compression is in flight.
- Stop rrweb/mutation producers synchronously during `stop()` while allowing already queued async compression to drain and flush.
- Synchronously drain pending compression on `beforeunload` so sendBeacon can include queued replay events.
- Fall back to the existing synchronous `fflate` path when native gzip is unsupported or fails.
- Export `isGzipSupported` from `@posthog/core`'s main entrypoint.
- Add unit tests for the async native gzip path, the synchronous fallback path, queued event ordering, stop-time queue draining, and beforeunload sync draining.

## Release info Sub-libraries affected

### Libraries affected

- [ ] All of them
- [x] posthog-js (web)
- [ ] posthog-js-lite (web lite)
- [ ] posthog-node
- [ ] posthog-react-native
- [ ] @posthog/react
- [ ] @posthog/ai
- [ ] @posthog/convex
- [ ] @posthog/next
- [ ] @posthog/nextjs-config
- [ ] @posthog/nuxt
- [ ] @posthog/rollup-plugin
- [ ] @posthog/webpack-plugin
- [ ] @posthog/types

## Checklist

- [x] Tests for new code
- [x] Accounted for the impact of any changes across different platforms
- [x] Accounted for backwards compatibility of any changes (no breaking changes!)
- [x] Took care not to unnecessarily increase the bundle size

### If releasing new changes

- [x] Ran `pnpm changeset` to generate a changeset file
prs.title: feat: use async gzip for replay event compression

prs.body:
## Summary

Adds three subpath entry points on `posthog-js` so downstream consumers can pull rrweb out of `posthog-js` instead of installing the now-deprecated standalone `@posthog/rrweb*` npm packages:

- `posthog-js/rrweb` → `@posthog/rrweb` (Replayer, record, etc.)
- `posthog-js/rrweb-types` → `@posthog/rrweb-types`
- `posthog-js/rrweb-plugin-console-record` → `@posthog/rrweb-plugin-console-record`

Uses the existing sibling-`package.json` pattern already in place for `posthog-js/react` so node's standard `main`/`module`/`types` resolution handles the new paths, without touching the top-level `exports` field (which would have risked breaking the established `posthog-js/dist/*` imports downstream).

Also ships the rrweb image-bitmap web worker sourcemap (`image-bitmap-data-url-worker-*.js.map`) from `posthog-js/dist/` via a small postbuild step, so downstream bundlers no longer need to reach into `node_modules/@posthog/rrweb` to satisfy the worker's `//# sourceMappingURL` reference.

### Build pipeline tweaks

- `packages/browser/rollup.config.mjs`: three new entries (`rrweb.es.ts`, `rrweb-types.es.ts`, `rrweb-plugin-console-record.es.ts`) feed into `rollup-plugin-dts` with `respectExternal: true` so their `.d.ts` outputs inline the underlying types instead of merely re-exporting them.
- The same config adds a `renderChunk` plugin that rewrites the `RRNodeType.` → `NodeType.` reference in the bundled `rrweb*.d.ts` files. This works around an existing type-generation quirk in `rrdom` where `NodeType` is imported as the alias `RRNodeType` but then referenced as a value (`readonly RRNodeType = RRNodeType.CDATA`), which gets lost during dts inlining.
- `packages/browser/scripts/copy-rrweb-worker-maps.js` copies the worker sourcemap from `@posthog/rrweb/dist` into `packages/browser/dist/` as a postbuild step. The new `files` entries on `packages/browser/package.json` make sure the new subpath `package.json` files, the dist sourcemap, and the entrypoint outputs are all included in the published tarball.

### Changeset

Includes a `posthog-js: minor` changeset describing the new subpath entries.

## Test plan

- [ ] CI green on this branch
- [ ] Manually verified locally that `import { Replayer } from 'posthog-js/rrweb'`, `import type { eventWithTime } from 'posthog-js/rrweb-types'`, and `import { LogLevel } from 'posthog-js/rrweb-plugin-console-record'` resolve from a Node.js ESM smoke test and that the bundled `.d.ts` files type-check.
- [ ] After publish, bump `posthog-js` in the `posthog` repo's catalog so the companion PR ([PostHog/posthog ahillen/consume-rrweb-from-posthog-js](https://github.com/PostHog/posthog/pulls?q=is%3Apr+head%3Aahillen%2Fconsume-rrweb-from-posthog-js)) can ship.

Made with [Cursor](https://cursor.com)
prs.title: feat(replay): expose rrweb subpath entries on posthog-js

prs.body:
## Problem

`posthog-js` already lets users tune the rage-click detector via `posthog.init({ rageclick: { threshold_px, timeout_ms, click_count } })`, but those values are baked in at SDK initialization time. There's no path to adjust detector sensitivity at the project level without shipping an SDK upgrade and getting customers to redeploy.

## Changes

- Add `RageclickRemoteConfig` (a subset of `RageclickConfig` covering only the three sensitivity knobs) and `RemoteConfig.rageclick?: RageclickRemoteConfig` so the SDK accepts these tunables from the `/array/{token}/config` response. Element/content ignore lists are intentionally not exposed remotely — they reference app-specific selectors and copy.
- Extract the detector's config-application from `RageClick`'s constructor into `RageClick.updateConfig(...)` so it can be re-applied after init. The in-flight click buffer is reset on every update so stale clicks don't fire against new thresholds.
- In `Autocapture.onRemoteConfig`, merge `response.rageclick` onto the live detector via `_applyRemoteRageclickConfig`. Per-field client config wins (a customer can pin one threshold in code and let the others come from the server), and `posthog.init({ rageclick: false })` is never re-enabled by the server.
- New tests in `rageclick.test.ts` covering `updateConfig` behavior, and end-to-end tests in `autocapture.test.ts` covering:
  - custom thresholds passed at init still work,
  - remote config tunables are applied to the live detector,
  - per-field client precedence over remote values,
  - `rageclick: false` locally is never overridden by remote,
  - absent `rageclick` on the remote config leaves the detector untouched.
- Added a `posthog-js: minor` changeset.

**Scope note:** Backend delivery of these tunables isn't shipped yet — this PR is the SDK-side wiring so the server side can land independently. The new `RemoteConfig.rageclick` field is optional and a no-op until the server starts populating it.

## Release info Sub-libraries affected

### Libraries affected

- [ ] All of them
- [x] posthog-js (web)
- [ ] posthog-js-lite (web lite)
- [ ] posthog-node
- [ ] posthog-react-native
- [ ] @posthog/react
- [ ] @posthog/ai
- [ ] @posthog/convex
- [ ] @posthog/next
- [ ] @posthog/nextjs-config
- [ ] @posthog/nuxt
- [ ] @posthog/rollup-plugin
- [ ] @posthog/webpack-plugin
- [ ] @posthog/types

## Checklist

- [x] Tests for new code
- [x] Accounted for the impact of any changes across different platforms
- [x] Accounted for backwards compatibility of any changes (no breaking changes!)
- [x] Took care not to unnecessarily increase the bundle size

### If releasing new changes

## Test plan

Made with [Cursor](https://cursor.com)

prs.body:
## Problem

context https://posthog.slack.com/archives/C0A3UEVDDNF/p1779372767858499?thread_ts=1779372288.067589&cid=C0A3UEVDDNF

PR authors can accidentally combine a `fix:` title with a `minor` changeset, which means the release bump does not match the Conventional Commit intent. The existing local versioning workflow also had a narrow filename now that it covers more than `posthog-js` major bumps.

## Changes

- Rename the local workflow to `validate-versioning.yml` to match its broader scope.
- Extend the workflow to flag `fix` PRs that include `minor` changesets.
- Post or update a PR comment explaining whether to change the changeset to `patch` or retitle the PR as `feat:`.
- Re-run the check on PR title edits and clean up the mismatch comment once resolved.
- Report all `posthog-js` major-bump changeset files if more than one is present.

## Release info Sub-libraries affected

### Libraries affected

- [ ] All of them
- [ ] posthog-js (web)
- [ ] posthog-js-lite (web lite)

prs.body:
Bumps the pinned SHA of `PostHog/posthog-sdk-test-harness/.github/workflows/test-sdk-action.yml` from `1b56b38f…` to current main HEAD `39d05346…`.

The old SHA predates
posthog-sdk-test-harness#18 (pinact rollout) which SHA-pinned the internal `actions/checkout@v4` reference. The org-wide "Require actions to be pinned to a full-length commit SHA" policy applies inside reusable
  workflows.
prs.title: chore(ci): bump pinned posthog-sdk-test-harness SHA

prs.body:
Bumps the pinned SHA of PostHog/.github reusable workflows from `d2e7c952` to `b6cef416`.

The old SHA predates PostHog/.github#40 which SHA-pinned the internal
`actions/create-github-app-token` and `actions/github-script` calls. The org-wide 'Require actions to be pinned to a full-length commit SHA' policy applies inside reusable workflows too, so any workflow still
  pinning the old SHA fails at runtime.
prs.title: chore(ci): bump pinned PostHog/.github reusable workflow SHA

Updates `ws` from 8.19.0 to 8.20.1
Release notes

Sourced from ws's releases.

8.20.1

Bug fixes

  • Fixed an uninitialized memory disclosure issue in websocket.close() (c0327ec1).

Providing a TypedArray (e.g. Float32Array) as the reason argument for websocket.close(), rather than the supported string or Buffer types, caused uninitialized memory to be disclosed to the remote peer.

import { deepStrictEqual } from 'node:assert';
import { WebSocket, WebSocketServer } from 'ws';

const wss = new WebSocketServer(
{ port: 0, skipUTF8Validation: true },
function () {
const { port } = wss.address();
const ws = new WebSocket(ws://localhost:${port}, {
skipUTF8Validation: true
});


ws.on('close', function (code, reason) {
  deepStrictEqual(reason, Buffer.alloc(80));
});



}
);


wss.on('connection', function (ws) {
ws.close(1000, new Float32Array(20));
});

The issue was privately reported by Nikita Skovoroda.

8.20.0

Features

  • Added exports for the PerMessageDeflate class and utilities for the Sec-WebSocket-Extensions and Sec-WebSocket-Protocol headers (d3503c1f).
Commits

  • c0327ec [security] Fix uninitialized memory disclosure in websocket.close()

  • 58e45b8 [ci] Do not test on node 25
  • 5f26c24 [ci] Run the lint step on node 24
  • 8439255 [dist] 8.20.0

Updates `ws` from 8.20.0 to 8.20.1
Release notes

Sourced from ws's releases.

8.20.1

Bug fixes

  • Fixed an uninitialized memory disclosure issue in websocket.close() (c0327ec1).

Providing a TypedArray (e.g. Float32Array) as the reason argument for websocket.close(), rather than the supported string or Buffer types, caused uninitialized memory to be disclosed to the remote peer.

import { deepStrictEqual } from 'node:assert';
import { WebSocket, WebSocketServer } from 'ws';

const wss = new WebSocketServer(
{ port: 0, skipUTF8Validation: true },
function () {
const { port } = wss.address();
const ws = new WebSocket(ws://localhost:${port}, {
skipUTF8Validation: true
});


ws.on('close', function (code, reason) {
  deepStrictEqual(reason, Buffer.alloc(80));
});



}
);


wss.on('connection', function (ws) {
ws.close(1000, new Float32Array(20));
});

The issue was privately reported by Nikita Skovoroda.

8.20.0

Features

  • Added exports for the PerMessageDeflate class and utilities for the Sec-WebSocket-Extensions and Sec-WebSocket-Protocol headers (d3503c1f).
